reasoning: This 24-bit sigma-delta ADC operating at 256 kSPS per channel is evaluated against 3A001.a.5, which controls ADCs based on resolution and output rate. For ADCs with 16 bits or more of resolution, the control threshold is an output rate exceeding 65 MSPS (3A001.a.5.a.5). At 256 kSPS (0.256 MSPS), this device's sample rate is far below the 65 MSPS threshold. It does not meet any other controlled parameter under 3A001.a.5 or other CCL entries, so it is classified as EAR99.

item_name: Quad RF-DAC / quad RF-ADC mixed-signal front-end
reasoning: This mixed-signal front-end IC integrates high-performance ADC and DAC converters controlled under 3A001.a. The four ADC channels operating at 4 GSPS exceed the controlled thresholds in 3A001.a.14 regardless of resolution (e.g., at 12-bit resolution, the 1.0 GSPS threshold of 3A001.a.14.c is far exceeded; even at 8-bit, the 3.6 GSPS threshold of 3A001.a.14.a is exceeded). The four DAC channels at 12 GSPS with high resolution would also be controlled under 3A001.a.5.b (settling time for ≥12-bit resolution). For this integrated device, 3A001.a.14 is cited as the primary controlling entry based on the ADC performance being clearly above all applicable thresholds.

item_name: 6 GSPS RF digital-to-analog converter, to 5 GHz
reasoning: This 16-bit DAC with a 6 GSPS (6,000 MSPS) update rate is controlled under 3A001.a.5.a, which captures digital-to-analog converters with a resolution of 14 bits or more and an adjusted update rate exceeding 400 MSPS. At 16 bits the resolution clearly meets the ≥14-bit threshold, and 6,000 MSPS far exceeds the 400 MSPS limit, placing it squarely within this controlled parameter.

item_name: Wideband 2x2 RF agile transceiver, 47 MHz-6 GHz
reasoning: This integrated RF transceiver IC (resembling devices like the AD9361) was evaluated against multiple CCL entries. Under 3A001.a.5, its 12-bit ADCs at ~61.44 MSPS are well below the controlled threshold of 400 MSPS for 12-bit resolution. Under 3A001.b.2 (MMICs), while it operates up to 6 GHz, it does not meet controlled parameters for power amplifier output power or low-noise amplifier noise figure at specified frequencies. Under 3A002.h, its PLL-based frequency synthesizer switching times (typically microseconds) do not meet the controlled fast-switching thresholds. Under 5A001.b, controls apply to complete telecommunications systems/equipment, not individual transceiver ICs. The 56 MHz channel bandwidth does not meet the 500 MHz threshold in 5A001.b.4. The device's technical parameters — frequency range (47 MHz–6 GHz), channel bandwidth (56 MHz), converter resolution/speed, and transmit power levels — are all within commercially common ranges and do not exceed any specific CCL-controlled thresholds, making it EAR99.

This 300 MHz single-core 32-bit audio DSP was evaluated against 3A001.a.5 (Digital Signal Processors). The controlled thresholds under 3A001.a.5 require aggregate throughput (APP) exceeding levels well above what a single-core 32-bit processor at 300 MHz can achieve (likely well under 1 GFLOPS/GOPS). The device is a commercial-grade audio-specific DSP designed for consumer/professional audio tasks (mixing, filtering, EQ) and does not meet the performance parameters necessary for control under 3A001. It also does not fall under 3A991 entries for specific controlled-but-lower-tier components. Therefore, it is subject to the EAR but not described by any specific CCL entry, warranting an EAR99 classification.

item_name: Narrow-band sub-1 GHz ISM RF transceiver
reasoning: This is a standard commercial narrow-band sub-1 GHz ISM-band RF transceiver IC using conventional FSK/GFSK modulation for low-power wireless data links and metering. It does not meet the controlled thresholds under 5A001 (e.g., no spread spectrum capabilities, no military-specific design, no controlled encryption beyond mass-market standards). It operates in common commercial ISM frequency bands with standard modulation techniques. Category 3 entries (3A001) for electronic components also do not capture this device, as it lacks the controlled parameters (e.g., specialized processing speed, radiation hardening, etc.). As a widely available commercial transceiver component not described by any specific CCL entry, it is properly classified as EAR99.

item_name: Integrated dual-channel RF transceiver, 75 MHz-6 GHz
reasoning: This highly-integrated dual-channel RF transceiver IC (similar to commercial devices like the AD9371/ADRV9009 family) was evaluated against several CCL entries. Under 3A001.b.2 (MMICs), its moderate output power (~7-10 dBm), moderate noise figure, and 200 MHz instantaneous bandwidth (only ~3.3% fractional bandwidth at 6 GHz) fall well below controlled thresholds for power amplifier, LNA, or wideband MMICs. Under 5A001.b.1.a (software defined radio), the Note explicitly decontrols equipment designed for civil cellular/wireless infrastructure use, which is the stated application. Under 3A002.h (ADC ICs), the embedded ADCs are integral to a transceiver function and not standalone converters of controlled performance. The device's specifications—commercially available, designed for standard wireless base-station and SDR infrastructure, with performance parameters below CCL thresholds—place it outside any specific CCL entry, making EAR99 the appropriate classification.

This SoC integrates a general-purpose ARM Cortex-A5 microprocessor core and two SHARC+ floating-point DSP cores. While it falls within the scope of Category 3 (Electronics), the performance characteristics of the ARM Cortex-A5 (relatively low CTP) and the SHARC+ DSP cores (commercial-grade, typically ~450 MHz) do not reach the controlled thresholds specified in 3A001.a.1 (microprocessor CTP limits) or 3A001.a.3 (DSP performance limits). As a general-purpose integrated circuit not meeting 3A001 parameters, it is properly classified under 3A991, which covers electronic devices and components not controlled by 3A001, including integrated circuits for commercial audio, industrial, and signal-processing applications.

This dedicated cryptographic controller IC is an integrated circuit specifically designed for 'information security' as defined in Category 5, Part 2. It employs digital cryptographic techniques (AES symmetric encryption, ECDSA/ECDH public-key operations, TLS acceleration) that perform cryptographic functions including confidentiality (AES bulk encryption, TLS session encryption, ECDH key agreement enabling confidentiality). These capabilities go beyond mere authentication or digital signature, placing the IC squarely under 5A002.a, which controls systems, equipment, and integrated circuits designed or modified to use cryptography employing digital techniques to perform cryptographic functions, including confidentiality. The secure key storage and TRNG further confirm its nature as a dedicated 'information security' component.

item_name: 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi + Bluetooth module, 4 MB flash
reasoning: This is a standard commercial 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi (802.11 b/g/n) and Bluetooth/BLE transceiver module. Wi-Fi incorporates WPA/WPA2 (AES-based) encryption and Bluetooth uses encryption, which would otherwise bring it under 5A002 (information security). However, as a widely available, mass-market wireless connectivity module (similar to ESP32-type modules), it qualifies for the mass-market encryption exclusion under Note 3 to Category 5, Part 2, and is therefore classified under 5A992.c as a mass-market encryption commodity controlled only for Anti-Terrorism (AT) reasons.

This low-power FPGA with approximately 3,520 logic cells and only 107 I/O pins is a small, low-cost device. FPGAs are evaluated under 3A001.a.7, which controls field programmable logic devices exceeding specific thresholds for single-ended digital I/O count, aggregate serial transceiver data rates, or equivalent gate counts. With only ~3,520 logic cells (roughly 35,000-70,000 equivalent 2-input gates depending on architecture) and 107 I/O pins, this device falls well below the controlled thresholds in 3A001.a.7 (which typically require far higher I/O counts and performance levels). The item is subject to the EAR but not described by any specific CCL entry, so it is classified as EAR99.

This secure element IC performs cryptographic functions beyond mere authentication/digital signature: it provides AES-128 encryption (a symmetric algorithm with a 128-bit key, exceeding the 56-bit threshold) and ECDH key agreement, in addition to ECDSA signing, SHA-256, and TRNG. Because the IC is designed to use cryptography employing digital techniques that perform encryption (a cryptographic function other than authentication or digital signature) using a symmetric algorithm with a key length exceeding 56 bits, it is controlled under 5A002.a.1.a.

This 16-bit digital signal controller operating at 60 MIPS is a commercially available, low-performance microcontroller (consistent with devices like the Microchip dsPIC33 family). The relevant CCL entries are 3A001.a.1 (microprocessor/microcontroller microcircuits) and 3A001.a.7 (digital signal processors). A 16-bit, 60 MIPS device yields an extremely low Composite Theoretical Performance (CTP), far below the controlled thresholds specified in 3A001.a.1.a (which requires CTP ≥ 5 WT or other elevated parameters). It also does not meet the performance thresholds for DSP control under 3A001.a.7. As the item is subject to the EAR but not described by any specific CCL entry at controlled thresholds, it is properly classified as EAR99.

This is a basic commercial-grade Layer-2 managed 10/100 Mbps Ethernet switch integrated circuit. Its operating speeds (10/100 Mbps) are well below any controlled thresholds in 5A001 (telecommunications) or 3A001 (electronics). It does not incorporate controlled encryption, does not meet performance parameters for controlled networking equipment, and has no features that would place it under any specific ECCN. While it could theoretically be considered under 5A991 as a telecommunications component, a standard commercial Ethernet switch IC at 10/100 Mbps with no special capabilities is most appropriately classified as EAR99 — subject to the EAR but not described by any specific CCL entry.

This is a standard commercial 10/100 Mbps Ethernet PHY transceiver implementing IEEE 802.3 at the physical layer. It operates at common, widely available data rates (10/100 Mbps), does not incorporate controlled encryption functionality (PHY layer only), and does not meet the performance thresholds or specialized characteristics described in Category 5 Part 1 entries such as 5A001 (e.g., no exceeding publicly available standards, no military-hardened specs). It is a mass-market commodity semiconductor component not described by any specific CCL entry.

A 10/100 Mbps Ethernet PHY transceiver is a standard commercial-grade networking component. Its data rates (10/100 Mbps) and functionality (basic physical-layer transceiver) fall well below the thresholds and technical parameters controlled under Category 5 Part 1 entries such as 5A001. It does not incorporate controlled encryption (Part 2), nor does it meet any other CCL entry criteria. As a widely available, commodity-grade integrated circuit for embedded networking, it is properly classified as EAR99.

The microcontroller integrates a hardware cryptographic engine performing AES and 3DES for data confidentiality. Both AES (128/192/256-bit keys) and 3DES (112/168-bit keys) employ symmetric key lengths in excess of 56 bits, meeting the threshold of 5A002.a.1. The SHA hash and RNG further support the cryptographic functionality. Category 5 Part 2 (Information Security) controls apply because the item is an integrated circuit specifically designed to use cryptography for data confidentiality with symmetric algorithms exceeding the 56-bit key length limit. If the item were demonstrated to qualify under the Cryptography Note (Note 3 to Cat 5 Part 2) as mass-market, it could be reclassified to 5A992.c, but based on the stated technical parameters alone, 5A002.a.1 is the controlling entry.

This is a standard, commercially available, certified Bluetooth Low Energy 4.2 radio module operating at 2.4 GHz ISM band. It does not meet the controlled technical parameters of 5A001 (e.g., no specialized spread spectrum, no HF/VHF operation, not a software-defined radio with controlled characteristics). While BLE 4.2 includes standard AES-128 encryption, the cryptographic functionality is ancillary to its primary function as a short-range wireless communications device and uses standard, publicly available Bluetooth protocol encryption, which would typically be excluded from 5A002 controls under Note 3 to Category 5 Part 2. As a mass-market, standard commercial short-range wireless transceiver module with no parameters exceeding CCL thresholds, it is properly classified as EAR99.

item_name: Quad-core ARM Cortex-A72 compute module (2 GB / 16 GB)
reasoning: This system-on-module is a digital computer/electronic assembly. The quad-core ARM Cortex-A72 at 1.5 GHz has an Adjusted Peak Performance (APP) far below the thresholds specified in 4A003 for controlled digital computers. As a computer not meeting 4A003 control parameters, it falls into 4A994 ('Digital computers, electronic assemblies, and related equipment, n.e.s., and specially designed components therefor'), which is controlled only for Anti-Terrorism (AT) reasons.

This is a general-purpose 32-bit ARM Cortex-M4 microcontroller (consistent with STM32F4 series) operating at 168 MHz. The relevant CCL entry is 3A001.a.1, which controls microprocessor and microcontroller microcircuits exceeding specific performance thresholds (e.g., aggregate peak performance, CTP). A single-core Cortex-M4 at 168 MHz with a simple FPU has a very low composite theoretical performance, well below the controlled thresholds in 3A001.a.1. It is a widely available, commercial-grade embedded microcontroller not described by any specific CCL entry, and is therefore classified as EAR99.

This is a standard 25.4 mm (1-inch) broadband dielectric mirror operating in the visible band (400-750 nm) on a fused-silica substrate. It does not meet the controlled parameters of any CCL entry. It is not a deformable mirror (6A004.a), does not meet the size or weight-to-area ratio thresholds for lightweight monolithic mirror blanks (6A004.b), is not space-qualified (6A004.c), and does not meet other controlled optical component specifications. This is a common commercial off-the-shelf optical component widely available from standard suppliers (e.g., Thorlabs, Edmund Optics). It is subject to the EAR but not described by any specific ECCN on the CCL, so it is properly classified as EAR99.

A standard kinematic mirror mount for 1-inch optics is a common, commercially available optomechanical component providing tip/tilt adjustment via micrometer screws. It does not meet the technical thresholds or descriptions of any specific CCL entry. Category 6 entries such as 6A004 cover specialized optical equipment (e.g., adaptive optics, space-qualified optics, deformable mirrors) but not generic laboratory mirror mounts. No other CCL category captures this item's characteristics. It is therefore classified as EAR99—subject to the EAR but not listed on the CCL.

This is a general-purpose microprocessor microcircuit. The relevant CCL entry for high-performance microprocessors is 3A001.a.1, which controls microprocessor microcircuits exceeding specified Composite Theoretical Performance (CTP) thresholds (e.g., 6.5 weighted TeraFLOPS). A single-core 32-bit ARM Cortex-A8 at 1 GHz has an estimated peak performance on the order of a few GFLOPS, which is far below the 3A001 CTP thresholds. Since it is a microprocessor microcircuit not controlled by 3A001, it falls under 3A991 ('Electronic devices and components not controlled by 3A001'), which covers microprocessor microcircuits below the 3A001 performance thresholds. Controlled for anti-terrorism (AT) reasons only.
